entry.title: Washington Post: 3 Jul, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Russia-Ukraine war live updates: Moscow claims capture of Lysychansk; Kyiv says Donbas battle ‘not over’
  • Russia claims capture of Lysychansk, key for control of Ukraine’s east
  • How green became the color of abortion rights
  • Wary of China threat, Taiwanese join Ukraine’s fight against Russia
  • Barriers to abortion in Canada make it an unlikely haven for Americans
  • A call to arms: Poland’s military and civilian defenses rethink the future
  • On the front lines, Ukrainian women are often the first responders
  • Kyiv disputes Russia’s claim that it has ‘completely surrounded’ Lysychansk
  • She helped get Trump elected. Now she’s raising crypto for Ukraine.
  • 4 reported dead after Alpine glacier chunk strikes hikers
  • China state museum opens in Hong Kong amid patriotism drive
  • How a favela in Rio got its clean water back, for $42,300
  • British theater, film director Peter Brook dies at age 97
  • Bus falls into deep ravine in southwest Pakistan, killing 19
  • Pope, unable to go to Congo, embraces Congolese in Rome
  • Russia claims capture of pivotal city in eastern Ukraine
  • Police: Children among 77 kept in Nigeria church for rapture
  • Heavy rains, floods prompt evacuations of Sydney suburbs
  • Israel's caretaker PM Lapid holds first Cabinet meeting
  • These high school seniors raced away from graduation to fight a fire
  • The Rainbow Family comes to Colorado, bringing peace, love and anxiety
  • Inflation is making homelessness worse
  • Bill to grant crypto firms access to Federal Reserve alarms experts
  • Texts, web searches about abortion have been used to prosecute women
  • Is There Anything That Gen Z Won’t Drink?
  • Are Things Really That Bad? Actually, No
  • Can Immigrants Save American Democracy?
  • To Save Ukraine, Slow Down on the Autobahn.
  • Why Japan Will Struggle to Do Without Russian Energy
  • The court’s EPA ruling was about something much bigger than one agency
  • It’s getting harder to define America’s character
  • Why Glenn Youngkin — or someone like him — must run in 2024
  • This July Fourth, we’re torn over what we love about our country
  • This 1972 speech is more relevant than ever this Fourth of July
  • Democracy must not die in darkness
  • Distinguished person of the week: She showed her party what patriotism is
  • July 4 is a time to sing our complicated country’s praises
  • Ask Eugene Robinson about the latest news around the U.S. and world
  • Chat with Perry Bacon Jr. about politics and beyond
  • Chat with Jennifer Rubin about her columns, politics, policy and more
  • Send a letter to the editor
  • Contact the reader representative
  • Submit an op-ed
Breaking news and analysis on politics, business, world national news, entertainment more. In-depth DC, Virginia, Maryland news coverage including traffic, weather, crime, education, restaurant reviews and more.

entry.title: NY Times: 3 Jul, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • The Long Path to Reclaim Abortion Rights
  • ‘The Pro-Life Generation’: Young Women Fight Against Abortion Rights
  • Texas Supreme Court Blocks Order That Allowed Abortions to Resume
  • Investigators of War Crimes in Ukraine Face Formidable Challenges
  • How a Military Base in Illinois Helps Keep Weapons Flowing to Ukraine

  • 76 Fake Charities Shared a Mailbox. The I.R.S. Kept Approving More.
  • 3-D Printing Grows Beyond Its Novelty Roots
  • The Democrat Who’s Flipping the Campaign Script
  • The Far Right and Far Left Agree on One Thing: Women Don’t Count
  • Abortion Bans Will Affect Americans Rich and Poor
  • More Americans Are Dying of Drug Overdoses Than Ever Before
  • An Ode to the Queen City of the Hudson
  • The American Flag Belongs to Me, Too, and This Year I’m Taking It Back
  • My O.C.D. Diagnosis Was a Blessing, Until it Became Too Central to My Life
  • Behind the Scenes of the Supreme Court
  • Running Post-Covid: How to Get Back to Training
  • Suing New York, Over and Over, for Transgender Rights
  • In Buffalo, New Apartments Sprout Up in Vacant Warehouses
  • Big Ben’s Bongs Will Soon Ring Out Again Across London
  • In Northern Ireland, Old Passions Are Smoldering Anew
  • Palestinians Give U.S. Bullet That Killed Shireen Abu Akleh
  • Ukraine War Volunteers Die in Battles Far From Home
  • Live Updates: Russia Claims to Seize City Key to Its Ambitions in Eastern Ukraine
  • Why Is There So Much Turmoil in Eastern Congo?
  • Abortion Laws in Europe, Canada, Mexico and Other Countries
  • Women who were raped by Russian soldiers yearn for justice.
  • In Estonia, Ethnic Russians Start Questioning Putin’s War
  • Lesia Tsurenko Plays Tennis to Earn Money to Help Ukraine
  • Miners Discover a Frozen Baby Mammoth in Canada
  • Ukraine Accuses Russia of Dropping White Phosphorous on Snake Island
  • A Guide to Fourth of July Weekend
  • Japan’s Secret to Taming the Coronavirus: Peer Pressure
  • Iran Earthquake Kills at Least 5, State Media Says
  • Denmark’s Leader Apologizes for Botched Pandemic Mink Cull
  • Uvalde Schools Police Chief Resigns From City Council
  • Florida Man Posing as Disney Worker Charged in Removal of R2-D2 at Hotel
  • Once a Crucial Refuge, ‘Gayborhoods’ Lose L.G.B.T.Q. Residents in Major Cities
  • Man Dies After Truck Propelled by Jet Engines Crashes at Michigan Air Show
  • Supreme Court Marshal Asks Officials to End Protests at Justices’ Homes
  • A Mother and Her 3 Children Are Found Dead in a Minnesota Lake
  • Akron Police Officers Placed on Leave After Fatal Shooting of Jayland Walker
  • Ex-Georgetown Tennis Coach Sentenced in Admissions Scandal
  • Supreme Court’s E.P.A. Ruling Shifts More Power Away From Congress
  • Tropical Storm Colin Forms Near Coast of South Carolina
  • Thousands of Flights Are Delayed or Canceled as July 4 Travel Kicks Off
  • Tesla Sales Slow as the Pandemic Hobbles Production
  • The Week in Business: Spending More, Getting Less
  • A Clunky Mask May be the Answer to Airborne Disease and N95 Waste
  • Bruce Katz, Pioneer of the Walking Shoe, Is Dead at 75
  • Post-Roe, Women Discover How Their Employer Influences Abortion Access
  • Baltimore Banner, a News Start-Up, Aims to Challenge The Sun
  • Sun Valley Conference 2022: When Private Jets Land in Small-Town Idaho
News from the New York Times

entry.title: Guardian: 3 Jul, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • F1 British Grand Prix: red flag after early crash at Silverstone – live updates
  • England v India: fifth Test, day three – live!
  • Tour de France 2022: day three updates – live!
  • Wimbledon: Norrie and Alcaraz in action, Watson out, Djokovic to come – live!
  • Russia-Ukraine war: at least six killed in attack on Slovyansk; Moscow claims troops have encircled Lysychansk – live
  • Liz Cheney won’t rule out criminal referral against Donald Trump
  • Boris Johnson took official jet home from weekend with family in Cornwall
  • ‘TurkAegean’ tourism campaign draws angry response from Athens
  • Amber Heard’s attorneys request defamation verdict be tossed
  • I’ve had crushes all my life, but I’m getting married soon – and making a promise to love just one person | Nell Frizzell
  • UN secretary general urges calm in Libya as protests spread
  • Cristiano Ronaldo’s departure will hurt but now Manchester United can move forward | Jonathan Wilson
  • The US norms the supreme court targeted this term all came from the same era | Bill McKibben and Akaya Windwood
  • Russia-Ukraine war: what we know on day 130 of the invasion
  • Goodbye silicone? A new era of breast reconstruction is on the horizon
  • Senior official criticises EU handling of Poland’s Covid recovery plan
  • Judge puts Biden on the spot over immunity for Saudi crown prince
  • Women’s Euro 2022 team guide No 14: France
  • ‘It’s a model of how to live’: Mandy Moore, star of This Is Us, on motherhood and music
  • Fresh row as Israel to conduct forensic tests on bullet that killed Shireen Abu Aqleh
  • Women’s rights have suffered a grim setback. But history is still on our side | Rebecca Solnit
  • Peter Brook: the great seeker of British theatre
  • Britain’s Notre Dame? Why MPs need to solve the Houses of Parliament problem
  • Peter Brook, influential theatre visionary, dies aged 97
  • Nigel Slater’s recipes for ricotta cake, and for roast tomatoes with thyme and garlic toasts
  • ‘Each day is agony’: the lives swept up in El Salvador’s mass arrests
  • England stuck in identity crisis and slipping backwards under Eddie Jones | Robert Kitson
  • Sydney floods: residents brace for ‘long night ahead’ as torrential downpour batters region
  • Double take: the weird and wonderful world of celebrity lookalikes
  • ‘People want me dead’: abortion providers fear violence after Roe overturned
  • Foreign Office to pay £423,000 to whistleblowing lawyer who lost job
  • Medics battle relentless wave of trauma in Ukrainian town of Borodyanka
  • Letters show how Boris Johnson backed return of Parthenon marbles
  • Yes, Nato has a new vitality. But its united front could collapse when it has to deal with Russia | Jonathan Eyal
  • Dr Zhivago’s heroine takes centre stage in plagiarism row
  • Nick Cave: Seven Psalms review – yearning for mercy and grace
  • Off-the-scale scrumptuous caramels
  • Scotland’s future won’t be settled until there is a second vote on independence | Andrew Rawnsley
  • George Russell: ‘I want to beat Lewis but that won’t make my season a success’
  • Explosion of life on Earth linked to heavy metal act at planet’s centre
  • Explosive testimony suggests Trump was set on a coup that evokes America’s darkest hour | Sarah Churchwell
  • Adele tells of guilt over cancelled Las Vegas shows, break with her father and being a ‘sad person’
  • Thibaut Pinot still France’s favourite but hopes of home glory thin on the ground | William Fotheringham
  • Feminist magazine Ms turns 50: a beacon for rights, sex equality and Roe v Wade
  • Is your smartphone ruining your memory? A special report on the rise of ‘digital amnesia’
  • The Nigerian gas deal, the Irish impresario and the £8bn ruling amid claims of bribery
  • Mob-style killings shock Netherlands into fighting descent into ‘narco state’
  • Mickey Mouse could soon leave Disney as 95-year copyright expiry nears
  • Meeting of Afghan clerics ends with silence on education for girls
  • As Trump’s star wanes, another rises: could Ron DeSantis be the new Maga bearer?
  • Germany and Ireland denounce Boris Johnson’s bid to ditch Northern Ireland protocol
  • The big picture: the persistence of normality in the Democratic Republic of the Congo
  • Daisy Haggard: ‘Forget work. Let’s just talk about Wotsits’
  • Sunday with Ardal O’Hanlon: ‘I often don’t get out of my tennis gear’
  • With July comes a whole new gardening agenda
  • Ryanair strike threat set to add to summer airport chaos in Europe
  • Should I dump my rich friends?
  • India tailor murder: police arrest two alleged ‘masterminds’
  • ‘It feels amazing’: revellers young and old celebrate Pride in London

entry.title: South China Morning Post: 3 Jul, 2022
entry.summary: South China Morning Post: 3 Jul, 2022 published at 2022-07-03 14:49PM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • ‘Hong Kong, add oil’ should not be taboo, says Canto-pop legend Jacky Cheung after mainland backlash over anniversary greeting
  • Alex Law, famed Hong Kong director and screenwriter behind Echoes of the Rainbow and An Autumn’s Tale, dies at age 69
  • Ni Kuang, celebrated Hong Kong novelist, dies at age 87
  • Coronavirus: Hong Kong lawmaker tests positive 2 days after posing for photo with Xi Jinping during 25th anniversary of handover celebrations
  • Plastic shopping bag charge should be increased to at least HK$2, says Hong Kong environmental group ahead of Legco meeting on levy
  • Chances of survival for 27 missing crew members of typhoon-hit vessel ‘very slim’, Hong Kong rescue authorities admit on day 2 of search
  • Hong Kong police lay holding charge of murder on man suspected of killing 23-year-old yoga instructor at 5-star hotel
  • Hong Kong leader John Lee convenes first cabinet meeting to discuss to-do list from Xi Jinping, vows to lobby for ‘limited measures’ to reopen border
  • Thousands show up for grand opening of Hong Kong Palace Museum to see 900 precious relics and national treasures on loan from Beijing
  • Hong Kong Sevens must be a proper comeback party, or we should wait
  • Can Hong Kong’s Park Lane shopping strip come back to life? Owners hope adding food, drinks will help
  • Hong Kong cancels all typhoon warning signals as Chaba weakens
  • Confucius, the opera: Hong Kong composer Gustav Mak mixes Western, Chinese elements in three-hour tribute to sage
  • Hong Kong’s new youth minister vows to quickly create blueprint to help disaffected young people, but admits progress will not be easy
  • Former Russian foreign minister warns ‘self-centred’ Nato risks deeper crisis over Ukraine
  • Chinese police officer’s false evidence claims raise fresh concerns about investigation into attack on female diners
  • Former Japanese prime minister urges Tokyo to talk more to China and stop trying to ‘encircle’ it alongside the US
  • Chinese food firm Maiquer under investigation over milk additive
  • After brutal attack, Chinese women are turning to self-defence classes
  • Climate change: melting Tibetan glaciers could unleash potentially dangerous bacteria, scientists say
  • Covid-19: new cases on the rise in China as travel rules ease
  • End of ‘Angola model’ sees number of Chinese in oil-rich African country plummet
  • Alibaba forms new data intelligence services subsidiary to deepen e-commerce giant’s pivot into enterprise market
  • How the crackdown on China’s top influencers is shaking up the once-booming live-streaming e-commerce industry
  • Could Chinese demand soften the blow of a US recession on Hong Kong, South Korea and Singapore?
  • Chinese ride-hailing giant Didi still awaits final ruling from Beijing, one year after it was put under cybersecurity review
  • Tesla’s second quarter sales drop amid supply chain issues and China pandemic restrictions
  • Indonesian historian Onghokham’s ‘fearless’ life as gay Chinese man, Java expert celebrated in biography
  • Set in Bali, filmed in Australia: Ticket to Paradise starring George Clooney, Julia Roberts, Maxime Bouttier sparks debate on ‘colonial gaze’
  • India’s war on meat could be critical for PM Modi’s BJP in the 2024 election
  • Awkward Boris Johnson-Jacinda Ardern handshake goes viral: ‘It looked like he was trying to tear her arm off’
  • Malaysia’s PM Ismail Sabri Yaacob faces mounting pressure to call snap vote, as Najib and allies step up Umno faction war
  • Russia claims control of last major Ukrainian city in eastern Luhansk
  • Legendary British theatre director Peter Brook dies aged 97
  • Swedish company develops plant-based ‘human meat burger’
  • How the US Supreme Court that Donald Trump filled is changing American life
  • Microplastics found in humans’ lungs, kidneys, spleen, placenta: ‘They’re everywhere’
  • Zelensky calls for international help to help rebuild Ukraine after war with Russia
  • Macedonians protest against French proposals for solving EU rift with Bulgaria
  • Afghan clerics’ assembly urges recognition of Taliban government
  • Israel says it has shot down Hezbollah drones headed for Mediterranean gas rig
  • Ukraine mayor accuses Russia of using banned cluster bombs, killing four
  • Thousands sign petition demanding removal of Supreme Court judge after Roe vs Wade abortion ruling
  • Jerry Hall files for divorce from Rupert Murdoch, asks for spousal support
  • Pride parade returns in London on 50th anniversary of first parade
  • Typhoon dampens property investor spirits as few flats in new Hong Kong housing project find buyers
  • Emerging Asia at risk from EU’s protectionist carbon border tax
  • Hong Kong’s dry spell for new stock offerings set to end with big China IPO deals
  • Nio, XPeng and Li Auto reported healthy sales of electric cars in June as they emerge from Covid-19-induced disruption
  • Climate change: Hong Kong bus operator KMB seeks government support to power rapid conversion of fleet to electric vehicles
  • Change shouldn’t intimidate Hong Kong – we have always thrived on it
  • Chinese art museum’s European offshoot in Berlin closes, a victim of social and political turbulence and funding issues, its artistic director says
  • Blind Chinese make-up artist teaches thousands of visually impaired women to apply make-up using sense of touch
  • A Star Ferry sunset tour of Hong Kong’s Victoria Harbour aboard converted vessel the World Star – what’s it like?
  • Gender-fluid Miss Universe contestant on having a ‘gay aura’ and why Bhutan should be seen as an LGBTQ-friendly country
  • How Hong Kong craft brewery became a passion project for co-founder Scott Powrie, and his ambition to climb some of the world’s highest peaks
  • How Hong Kong basketball star playing in US has changed in just 1 year – she now exudes self-confidence and gets much more sleep
  • Where ‘plastic’ came from, its crafty origins and how it became the dirty word it is today
  • Why trans women opt for gender confirmation surgery in Thailand whatever the cost, when procedures can be much cheaper, or free, at home
  • The man who can answer any question on China you might have, and why ‘Have you looked in Wilkinson?’ will remain the common response to a Chinese historical query
  • Fallen Angels: Wong Kar-wai on casting Leon Lai, Michele Reis, Charlie Yeung and Karen Mok against type in 1995 film
  • 8 most romantic restaurants in Hong Kong: from Michelin-starred Écriture and Tirpse, to knockout views atop the ICC at Odyssée and Tosca di Angelo sure to dazzle any date
  • How Chinese crypto king Changpeng Zhao lost US$77.5 billion: the Binance CEO went from boom to bust amid the bitcoin and NFT crash, but luckily doesn’t ‘really care much about money’
  • Meet Karan Adani, son of Asia’s richest man Gautam Adani: the 35-year-old CEO of APSEZ is set to inherit Adani Group and has been ‘groomed’ for his dad’s multibillion-dollar empire since 2009
  • Meet Elvis Presley’s ‘stepson’ Navarone Garibaldi: he’s Priscilla’s son with her second husband and Lisa Marie’s half-brother and only sibling ... and also a singer in a rock band
SCMP.com, Hong Kong's premier online English daily provides exclusive up-to-date news, audio video news, podcasts, RSS Feeds, Blogs, breaking news, top stories, award winning news and analysis on Hong Kong and China.

  • Japan’s JERA says it shut down 500-MW power plant due to fire
  • U.S. investment firm gets 1st refusal right over Sogo, Seibu sale
  • Russia’s control of Sakhalin project could pose upside risk to LNG prices
  • North Korea slams US-South Korea-Japan military cooperation
  • China’s top diplomat visits Myanmar amid opposition protest
  • Governor: Russians gaining foothold in pivotal Ukraine city
The Asahi Shimbun is widely regarded for its journalism as the most respected daily newspaper in Japan. The English version offers selected articles from the vernacular Asahi Shimbun, as well as extensive coverage of cool Japan,focusing on manga, travel and other timely news.

The Korea JoongAng Daily is an English-language daily published by the JoongAng Group, Korea’s leading media group, in association with The New York Times.
